cybersecurity.q: What makes WEX's cybersecurity practice different from a Big Four firm?
cybersecurity.a: Big Four security engagements typically involve senior partners who sell the work and junior analysts who execute it. At WEX, the expert you meet in the first conversation is the practitioner who leads your engagement. James Gorman brings 30+ years of hands-on security leadership across federal, healthcare, and financial-sector environments — that expertise is present throughout the engagement, not just in the pitch.

cybersecurity.q: Do you offer fractional CISO services?
cybersecurity.a: Yes. Our fractional CISO model gives organizations enterprise-grade security leadership at 40-60% of the cost of a full-time hire. This includes board-level reporting, regulatory alignment, vendor oversight, and strategic roadmap ownership. It is ideal for organizations that need the credibility and capability of a CISO without the full-time overhead.

cybersecurity.q: Which compliance frameworks do you work with?
cybersecurity.a: We cover the full range of enterprise compliance requirements: NIST CSF 2.0, HIPAA Security and Privacy Rules, PCI-DSS, SOC 2 Type I and II, ISO 27001, and GDPR data privacy. We also work with sector-specific requirements in healthcare, financial services, and government contracting. Our approach is to treat compliance as a byproduct of good security architecture — not a separate checkbox exercise.

cybersecurity.q: How do you approach incident response preparedness?
cybersecurity.a: We build readiness before the incident, not just response plans after one. That means developing your incident response plan, running tabletop exercises that simulate realistic breach scenarios, establishing communication and notification protocols, and aligning your IR program with your business continuity and disaster recovery planning. Teams that have practiced respond in hours instead of days.

cybersecurity.q: What is the starting point for a new cybersecurity engagement?
cybersecurity.a: Most engagements begin with our Technical Risk Assessment — a structured evaluation of your current security posture against the frameworks and risks most relevant to your organization. It produces a prioritized roadmap with specific, actionable recommendations. From there we can move into any of our practice areas based on where your highest-priority gaps are.

data-consulting.q: What does a data strategy engagement typically look like?
data-consulting.a: Most data strategy engagements start with a current-state assessment covering your data landscape, architecture maturity, governance gaps, and AI readiness. From there we build a prioritized roadmap tied to your specific business objectives — not a generic framework. Most clients leave with a 90-day execution plan and a 12-18 month strategic roadmap. Engagements typically run 4-8 weeks depending on scope.

data-consulting.q: We have a lot of data but nobody trusts it. Where do we start?
data-consulting.a: That is the most common problem we solve. Untrusted data is almost always a governance and quality problem, not a technology problem. We start with a data quality and governance assessment to identify where the trust breakdown is happening — whether it is inconsistent definitions, unclear ownership, pipeline reliability, or something else — and build a targeted remediation plan before recommending any new tooling.

data-consulting.q: Can you help us build an AI program from scratch?
data-consulting.a: Yes, and we will be honest with you about what you need first. Many organizations want to move directly to AI and skip the data foundation work. That almost always results in a failed pilot 6-12 months later. We run an AI Readiness Diagnostic that tells you exactly where your data, architecture, governance, and organizational readiness stand before you invest in AI tools. If you are ready, we build the program. If you are not, we fix what is blocking you first.

data-consulting.q: Do you work with specific cloud platforms or are you vendor-agnostic?
data-consulting.a: We are vendor-agnostic and deliberately so. We have deep expertise across AWS, Azure, and GCP data platforms, as well as the major data warehouse, lakehouse, and BI tools. Our recommendations are based on your workload requirements, existing investments, and long-term architecture goals — not partnership incentives.

data-consulting.q: How do you handle organizations with limited internal data teams?
data-consulting.a: Most of our mid-market clients do not have large internal data teams — that is a primary reason they come to us. We can operate as a full fractional data team, as an advisory layer on top of an existing team, or as a delivery partner during a buildout phase. We also focus heavily on knowledge transfer so that your internal capabilities grow alongside the programs we build.

digital-engineering.q: How is WEX different from a traditional IT consulting firm or Big Four implementation partner?
digital-engineering.a: The most important difference is who actually does the work. Traditional firms bring senior partners to sell, then staff your project with consultants who are learning on your budget. WEX operates with senior practitioners throughout — the architect who designs your cloud migration is the engineer who leads the execution. No bait and switch. No 1990s offshoring models. No bloated teams billing unnecessary hours.

digital-engineering.q: We have a cloud migration that has already been scoped. Can you take over the execution?
digital-engineering.a: Yes. We regularly inherit migrations that were scoped by another firm or that stalled during execution. We start with a rapid current-state assessment to validate the existing architecture decisions, identify any risks in the plan, and confirm the sequencing still makes sense. From there we take ownership of execution with clear milestones, risk checkpoints, and cutover management.

digital-engineering.q: What is your approach to technical debt?
digital-engineering.a: We treat technical debt as a business risk, not just an engineering problem. Before recommending modernization investments we quantify the operational cost, delivery risk, and security exposure of existing debt so leadership can make informed prioritization decisions. We build technical debt remediation into project delivery cycles rather than treating it as a separate initiative that never gets funded.

digital-engineering.q: Can you help us build a DevOps and CI/CD capability from scratch?
digital-engineering.a: Yes. We design and build CI/CD pipelines, infrastructure-as-code foundations, and internal developer platforms for organizations at all stages of DevOps maturity. Whether you are shipping monthly and want to ship weekly, or shipping weekly and want to ship daily, we build the delivery infrastructure that makes that velocity sustainable without sacrificing stability or compliance requirements.

digital-engineering.q: How do you handle intelligent automation projects — do you work with specific RPA platforms?
digital-engineering.a: We work across the major platforms including UiPath, Automation Anywhere, and Microsoft Power Automate, as well as AI-native orchestration tools. Platform selection is always driven by your existing tech stack, integration requirements, and total cost of ownership — not vendor preferences. We also ensure automation projects begin with process analysis and redesign so you are not just automating a broken process faster.

sales-improvement.q: What is the 101/202/303 model and how does it work in practice?
sales-improvement.a: It is WEX CRO Kurt Smith's core sales methodology. 101 is establishing context — earning the right to go deeper by demonstrating you understand the buyer's world better than they expect. 202 is surfacing the real problem — moving from symptoms to root causes and quantifying the cost of inaction. 303 is painting the vision — a concrete before-and-after story where the buyer is the hero and the path to that outcome is clear. The deal is simply agreeing on how to get there. This framework applies from first outreach through executive close.

sales-improvement.q: We have high rep turnover. Is this really a story problem?
sales-improvement.a: Often yes, at least partially. Reps who cannot tell a compelling story in front of a skeptical buyer lose confidence fast, struggle to build pipeline, and disengage within 12-18 months. Before attributing turnover entirely to compensation or culture, we recommend a GTM narrative audit. If reps cannot articulate why your product matters in the buyer's language, no amount of coaching will fix the retention problem.

sales-improvement.q: What does a GTM narrative engagement deliver at the end?
sales-improvement.a: A core messaging architecture that includes your primary narrative arc, persona-specific story variants, competitive differentiation language, a pitch deck built on the 101/202/303 framework, discovery conversation guides, and an objection-handling playbook. Everything is built to be used by reps in live conversations, not filed in a shared drive and forgotten.

sales-improvement.q: Do you work with specific CRM platforms for RevOps engagements?
sales-improvement.a: We work across Salesforce, HubSpot, and Microsoft Dynamics as primary CRM platforms, along with the surrounding RevOps tech stack including engagement platforms, conversation intelligence tools, and revenue analytics solutions. We lead with process and data architecture before making tool recommendations — a clean process in the wrong tool outperforms a broken process in the best tool.

sales-improvement.q: How long does a typical sales improvement engagement take to show results?
sales-improvement.a: Narrative and enablement work typically shows early signals within 60-90 days — improved discovery conversation quality, higher proposal-to-close rates, and faster rep ramp times. RevOps and pipeline structure improvements often show up in forecast accuracy and stage progression within one or two quarters. We set specific success criteria at the start of every engagement so you know exactly what to measure and when.

ai-agents.q: What is the difference between an AI agent and a chatbot?
ai-agents.a: A chatbot responds to questions. An AI agent takes actions. Agents can query systems, make decisions based on data, trigger workflows, escalate to humans when needed, and complete multi-step tasks autonomously. The critical difference is that agents operate with goal-directed autonomy inside your actual business systems — they are not just a Q&A interface layered on top of a knowledge base.

ai-agents.q: Our organization has tried AI pilots before and they never made it to production. Why is this different?
ai-agents.a: Most AI pilots fail because organizations skip the foundation work. Agents are only as reliable as the data they access, the systems they connect to, and the governance frameworks that define when they act autonomously versus escalate to humans. WEX starts every agentic AI engagement with a readiness assessment that identifies exactly what is blocking production deployment — and we do not move forward until those blockers are addressed.

ai-agents.q: How do you handle AI governance and compliance in regulated industries?
ai-agents.a: Governance is not an afterthought in our engagements — it is built into the architecture. For regulated industries like healthcare, financial services, and manufacturing, we design agents with explicit audit trails, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, explainability requirements, and data handling policies aligned to HIPAA, SOC 2, and other applicable frameworks. Every agent we deploy can demonstrate its reasoning and produce a compliance-ready record of its actions.

ai-agents.q: What departments typically see the best ROI from AI agents first?
ai-agents.a: Based on our engagements, the highest early ROI typically comes from finance and accounting (invoice processing, reconciliation, reporting), customer success and support (tier-1 resolution, escalation routing, account health monitoring), and sales operations (data enrichment, pipeline hygiene, activity logging). We use ROI modeling in our AI Readiness Diagnostic to identify your specific highest-leverage entry points before any build work begins.

ai-agents.q: Do we need to rebuild our data infrastructure before deploying agents?
ai-agents.a: Not always, but sometimes. It depends on the quality, accessibility, and governance maturity of your existing data. Some organizations can deploy targeted agents against well-structured, governed data sources right away. Others need foundational data work first. Our AI Readiness Diagnostic gives you a clear answer within weeks — not months of analysis paralysis.

calculators.q: How accurate are the calculator estimates?
calculators.a: The calculators use industry benchmarking data and typical ranges from our client engagements. They are designed to give you a directionally accurate sense of opportunity or cost — not a precise quote. Use them to validate whether a more detailed assessment is worth pursuing, then book a session with our team to get numbers specific to your environment and situation.

calculators.q: Why is there a range instead of a single number in the results?
calculators.a: Actual outcomes depend heavily on factors that vary by organization — current infrastructure state, team maturity, regulatory requirements, and implementation approach. We use ranges that reflect the realistic distribution of outcomes we have seen across similar engagements. The low end assumes more complexity and slower adoption; the high end reflects well-executed programs in favorable conditions.

calculators.q: What happens after I use a calculator?
calculators.a: The next step is a 30-minute strategy session with our team. We use the calculator output as a starting point to understand your specific situation, validate the assumptions, and determine whether a formal assessment makes sense. There is no obligation — the session is designed to give you actionable information regardless of whether you engage us further.
